moves.prompt:
You are playing Heads-Up Texas Hold'em Lite (no-limit style, one hand). Each player starts with 200 chips; small blind 1, big blind 2. You are dealt two private hole cards; five community cards are revealed across preflop, flop, turn, and river. Make the best five-card hand at showdown.
Actions:
  fold  - give up the hand
  check - pass (only when there is no bet to call)
  call  - match the current bet
  bet N - bet when no bet is facing you; N = your TOTAL chips committed this street
  raise N - raise a facing bet; N = your TOTAL chips committed this street (raise-TO)
  all_in - commit your entire remaining stack
N must be an integer within the stated legal range.
This is a MATCH, not a single hand: you play up to a fixed number of hands, your stack carries over between hands, and you win the match by finishing with more chips than your opponent (or if they bust). Manage risk across the whole match — protect a lead, take measured risk when behind.

Before you act, weigh your hand strength, your position, the pot odds, and what the betting so far suggests about your opponent.

Heads-Up MATCH — hand 1 of 30. Stacks carry across hands; you win the match by finishing with more chips than your opponent (or if they bust). Chips at the start of this hand — you: 200, opponent: 200 (you are even by 0).

Your committed this street: 1, opponent: 2. To call: 1.
Legal actions: fold, call, raise, all_in.
Raise-to amount (total this street) must be in [4, 200].

Choose exactly one legal action: fold, call, raise, all_in.
Respond with ONLY the action (and an integer amount for bet/raise), e.g. `call`, `check`, `fold`, `all_in`, `bet 6`, or `raise 12`. Think privately before you answer.

moves.thinking:
I have 9c Qh with board 9s 8d 2s Qc. I have two pair: Queens and Nines. This is a very strong hand.

Opponent raised my bet from 8 to 24. They could have a straight draw (T7, J7, JT), a flush draw (two spades), a set, or possibly a better two pair (unlikely given the board).

With top two pair, I should raise here. Let me think about sizing.

Pot is 42, I've put in 8, opponent put in 24. To call costs 16 more.

If I call, pot becomes 58.


If I raise to around 80, I'm building the pot with a strong hand while giving draws bad odds. That feels like the right move here.

moves.thinking:
I have 9c Qh. Board: 9s 8d 2s Qc 2c.

My hand: Two pair - Queens and Nines with a 2 kicker... actually, the board has 2s and 2c, so the board has a pair of 2s. My hole cards are 9c and Qh.

Best 5-card hand: Qh, Qc, 9c, 9s, 2s/2c = two pair Queens and Nines. Wait, with the pair of 2s on board, I have:
- Qh + Qc = pair of Queens


- 9c + 9s = pair of Nines
- 2s + 2c = pair of Twos on board

So my best hand is QQ99 with a 2 kicker - a very strong two pair. The only hand that beats me here is a full house, which my opponent would need three of a kind to make.

Let me think through what three of a kind they could have. They'd need a 9, Q, or 2 paired with what's on the board. But I'm holding Qh and have Qc on the board, so there are only two queens total - they can't have QQ. For nines, I have 9c and the board shows 9s, so that's two nines accounted for and they can't make three. Same with the twos - both are on the board, so they can't have a pair of twos for four of a kind. The only way they get a full house is if they're holding 9-2, giving them 99 and 22 for a full house with nines full of twos. 2 (board 2s, 2c + hole 2) = full house 2s full of Qs. That beats me!

If opponent has 8-2, they have 8,8 (board 8d + hole 8) and 2,2,2 = full house 2s full of 8s. That beats me!

If opponent has any x-2 (2 as hole card), they get full house.

Hmm, so any hand with a 2 beats me. But there are only 2 twos in the deck total (2s and 2c are on the board), so opponent CANNOT have a 2 in their hole cards since both 2s are on the board!

Right, both 2s are on the board (2s and 2c). So opponent cannot have a 2 in their hole cards.

Now I'm thinking about straights. With 9-8-Q-2-2 on the board, a straight would need specific cards like T-J to make 8-9-T-J-Q. If the opponent has T-J, that's a straight that beats my two pair. Given they called preflop and the flop, then raised the turn, that's worth considering.

The opponent's aggressive turn raise to 24 and then calling my re-raise to 80 suggests they have something strong. My QQ99 is solid, but the only hands that beat me are a straight like T-J, or possibly another two pair. The opponent could have Q9 for the same hand, which would split the pot, or 98 for 9988, but my QQ99 would still win that matchup.

With the pot now at 170 and stacks at 115 each, this is essentially a shove situation. Given how much action happened on the turn, I should be prepared to bet or shove on the river since my hand is very strong and the main threat is just the T-J straight.

But wait, I'm also realizing 88 is a real concern—if my opponent has pocket eights, they'd have a full house (eights full of twos) which beats my two pair. Since there's only one 8 on the board, they could definitely have that in their hole cards.

So really just TJ and 88 are the hands that beat me here. Given they called preflop and the flop bet, then raised the turn, a made straight or a set of eights both make sense for that line of play. When I re-raised to 80, they just called instead of shoving, which is interesting.

I'm going all-in for 115 into the 170 pot. They'd be getting about 40% odds to call, needing roughly 40% equity to make it worthwhile. If I'm ahead most of the time—which I should be with this two pair—they shouldn't call unless they're holding one of those two hands that beat me. This is the right spot to extract value.
